story_summary: Neighbors Blue and Ruby Cole acted as surrogate parents, offering time, protection, and practical help across decades; their long devotion culminated in Ruby leaving her estate to Sandy and Daddy, a surprising reward for years of care.

context.time_period: Mid-20th century through early 21st century (WWII years to 2008)
context.stakes: Emotional well-being and upbringing of children; caregiving and management of an elderly neighbor's affairs; unexpected inheritance

core_conflict: Tension between parental authority (mother's strict discipline and resentment) and the Coles' surrogate parenting/meddling, and later the responsibility of caring for and managing Ruby's affairs culminating in an unexpected estate distribution.

decision_moments.trigger: Narrator had begged Mother for a Radio Flyer wagon and Mother refused.
constraints: Social expectation not to interfere with another family's parenting
options: Respect mother's decision and do nothing
Convince mother to change her mind
decision_moments.choice: Blue bought the Radio Flyer and gave it to the child, driving him up and down the street.
decision_moments.reasoning: "Well, he wanted it, and I wanted him to have it. That's just all there is to it, Frances. You should just cool down!"
decision_moments.risk_accepted: Provoking Mother's anger and being seen as interfering in parental discipline
decision_moments.outcome: Child received the wagon and Blue shrugged off Mother's screaming.

decision_moments.trigger: Child accidentally hit Ruby in the head with a bat, causing a gash; Mother saw them returning from the doctor.
constraints: Presence of the child's mother who would likely punish the child
Immediate need to explain Ruby's injury
options: Tell the truth about the accident
Conceal the truth with an alternate story
decision_moments.choice: Ruby lied, saying she had stumbled down the back door stairs and hit her head on a concrete block.
decision_moments.reasoning: Fearing I would undoubtedly be punished, Ruby rapidly cooked up a tall tale of how she had stumbled going down the back door stairs and hit her head on a concrete block.
decision_moments.risk_accepted: Dishonesty and potential future trust implications
decision_moments.outcome: Child avoided punishment; Blue winked and smiled.

decision_moments.trigger: Ruby became feeble, nearly blind and deaf, and moved to a nursing home.
constraints: Responsibility for legal and practical tasks related to her household
options: Allow others (the state or nursing home) to manage her affairs
Provide occasional visits without managing affairs
Take primary responsibility for daily needs and estate management
decision_moments.choice: Sandy and Daddy provided regular visits, cared for her needs, disposed of household goods, sold the house, and managed her affairs.
decision_moments.reasoning: Sandy and Daddy looked after her every need. They visited her in the nursing home regularly. Daddy disposed of all her household goods and sold the house for her when she became too feeble to live there alone. Sandy managed all her personal affairs and kept her abreast of the local news.
decision_moments.risk_accepted: Time, emotional labor, and administrative burden
decision_moments.outcome: They cared for her needs and later inherited half of Ruby's sizable estate.

decision_moments.trigger: Daddy was disposing of Ruby's household items; narrator visited to look over remaining things.
constraints: Respect for the deceased's possessions and absence of close relatives
decision_moments.choice: Narrator took a small end table that contained the set of dominos.
decision_moments.reasoning: I decided to take a small end table that still contained the set of dominos that Blue and I had played with when I was a child.
decision_moments.risk_accepted: Possible perception of taking items without explicit permission
decision_moments.outcome: Narrator kept the domino table as a sentimental keepsake.

principles.principle: Consistent generosity of time and small acts builds deep, lasting emotional bonds.
principles.evidence: They both were extremely generous with their time, playing with us and teaching us things as we hung out at their house.

principles.principle: Long-term devotion and caregiving can lead to unexpected tangible rewards.
principles.evidence: When her will was read, it came to light that she had left her entire estate to be divided equally between Sandy and Daddy.

principles.principle: Protecting a child may justify shielding them from consequences, even by bending the truth.
principles.evidence: Fearing I would undoubtedly be punished, Ruby rapidly cooked up a tall tale of how she had stumbled going down the back door stairs and hit her head on a concrete block.

principles.principle: Small, everyday behaviors contribute to enduring legacies—both emotional and material.
principles.evidence: for the legacies they left for Sandy and me, both material and emotional gifts.

decision_heuristics.heuristic: If a child's simple wish brings clear joy and caregivers refuse, consider providing the gift directly to create lasting positive memory.
decision_heuristics.evidence: Well, he wanted it, and I wanted him to have it. That's just all there is to it, Frances. You should just cool down!

decision_heuristics.heuristic: When an elderly person becomes unable to manage, proactively visit regularly and assume practical responsibilities (dispose property, manage affairs) on their behalf.
decision_heuristics.evidence: Sandy and Daddy looked after her every need. They visited her in the nursing home regularly. Daddy disposed of all her household goods and sold the house for her. Sandy managed all her personal affairs

decision_heuristics.heuristic: If a child faces certain punishment from caregivers, quickly create a protective, plausible explanation to shield them.
decision_heuristics.evidence: Fearing I would undoubtedly be punished, Ruby rapidly cooked up a tall tale...

leadership_patterns.risk_tolerance: Moderate (willing to accept social/relational risk to protect or benefit others)
leadership_patterns.communication_style: Informal, protective, pragmatic—uses direct action, gentle deflection, and humor
leadership_patterns.accountability_posture: Proactive and hands-on—assumes responsibility for caregiving and practical tasks
leadership_patterns.ethical_framing: Relationally pragmatic (prioritizes care, loyalty, and protection over strict rule-following)

quotes.quote: Well, he wanted it, and I wanted him to have it. That's just all there is to it, Frances. You should just cool down!
Fearing I would undoubtedly be punished, Ruby rapidly cooked up a tall tale...
They both were extremely generous with their time, playing with us and teaching us things...
Sandy and Daddy looked after her every need.
When her will was read, it came to light that she had left her entire estate to be divided equally between Sandy and Daddy.
